summary: On the morning of September 11, 2001, I first learned about the attacks while getting coffee and saw on TV that a plane hit the World Trade Center. Soon after, I heard about the second plane hitting the towers, the Pentagon attack, and the crash in Pennsylvania. The day was chaotic, with bridges closing and emergency responses mobilizing. I spent the next three weeks involved in various rescue and security operations, witnessing the devastation and the massive community effort to help.

I was shocked when I saw on TV that a plane had hit the World Trade Center early on September 11. Later, I learned a second plane had struck the towers, and eventually, I was told the buildings collapsed. The World Trade Center was the focal point of the rescue and recovery efforts, and I was fortunate to be part of the team assisting at Ground Zero, helping with debris removal and supporting rescue workers.

On the morning of September 11, after hearing about the attacks on the World Trade Center, I also learned that a plane had hit the Pentagon. This added to the confusion and seriousness of the situation. The Pentagon attack was part of the coordinated terrorist strikes that day, and it heightened the sense of urgency and danger felt across the tri-state area.

I heard on the news that one of the planes involved in the September 11 attacks crashed into a field in Pennsylvania. This was part of the tragic series of events that unfolded that morning. The crash site in Pennsylvania was a grim reminder of the widespread impact of the attacks beyond New York and Washington, D.C.

New York City was at the heart of the September 11 attacks, with the World Trade Center being the main target. I was trying to get into the city that morning but faced bridge closures and heavy traffic. Once inside, I saw the massive emergency response and the city’s resilience. I spent many days working in and around New York City, supporting rescue and security operations.

I live near Staten Island and my route to work took me through there on September 11. Despite heavy traffic and bridge closures, I was able to get into Staten Island by showing my police credentials. The 122nd Precinct in Staten Island was active that day, rallying officers to assist. Staten Island was a key staging area for us before heading out on boat missions.

On my way to work on September 11, I took the Garden State Parkway and noticed many state police cruisers heading toward Jersey City and Bayonne to assist. The Parkway was busy with emergency vehicles, and it was clear the whole region was mobilizing. I exited the Parkway to take Route 440 into Staten Island amid heavy traffic.

Jersey City was one of the areas where state police were heading to assist on September 11. I saw many emergency vehicles en route there from the Garden State Parkway. It was part of the broader tri-state response to the attacks, with Jersey City playing a role in staging and support.

Bayonne was another location where state police were dispatched on September 11, as I observed while driving on the Garden State Parkway. The area was part of the coordinated emergency response in the region. Bayonne’s proximity to Staten Island and New York City made it an important point in the overall security and rescue efforts.

After leaving the Garden State Parkway, I took Route 440 heading into Staten Island on September 11. The traffic was bumper to bumper, but I used my police badge to clear a path by moving vehicles to the side. Route 440 was a critical access route for me to get to work and join the emergency response efforts that day.

I passed the 122nd Precinct in Staten Island on my way to work on September 11 and saw many NYPD officers rallying there. The precinct was an important hub for organizing police personnel to assist with the crisis. It was clear that law enforcement was mobilizing quickly to support the rescue and recovery efforts.

When I arrived at work on Staten Island on September 11, I was told to get dressed and stand by at Fort Wadsworth. It was a staging area for us before being assigned to details. From there, I was sent to the Coast Guard Station in Rosebank to await further instructions and boat assignments.

I have 10 years of experience in the United States Coast Guard, which helped me during the September 11 response. The Coast Guard set up command posts and coordinated marine operations in the harbor. I worked closely with them, especially when assigned to the Coast Guard Station in Rosebank and operating patrol boats to transport personnel and supplies.

On September 11, I was sent down to the Coast Guard Station in Rosebank, Staten Island, to wait for a boat to pick me up. It was just down the street from my workplace and served as a staging point for marine operations. From Rosebank, we launched patrol boats to assist with transporting injured personnel and supplies to and from Manhattan and Ellis Island.

Sergeant Dorino was the captain of one of our patrol boats on September 11. When I arrived at Rosebank, his boat came to pick us up for missions to Ellis Island and Manhattan. His leadership was crucial in navigating the harbor and supporting the rescue and security efforts during those chaotic days.

Ellis Island was used as a staging point on September 11 to bring in injured and sick people from the World Trade Center collapse. We transported medical personnel and supplies there and provided security for the island. It was a critical hub in the emergency response, and I spent a lot of time ferrying people and resources between Ellis Island and Manhattan.

Manhattan was ground zero for the September 11 attacks, and I made multiple trips there by boat to deliver supplies, doctors, and nurses. We also conducted security checks, such as inspecting the Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel air duct on Governors Island. Being in Manhattan during those days was humbling and showed the scale of destruction and the massive rescue efforts underway.

On September 11, we were assigned by the NYPD command post to check the huge air duct for the Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel located on Governors Island for explosives. This was part of the security measures to ensure no further threats were present. Thankfully, we found nothing suspicious, but it was an important precaution during those tense days.

Governors Island was where we conducted security checks on the Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel air duct after the attacks on September 11. It was a strategic location in the harbor and part of the broader security perimeter around Manhattan. The island was involved in the coordinated efforts to safeguard critical infrastructure.

The tri-state area, including New York, New Jersey, and parts of Pennsylvania, was deeply affected by the September 11 attacks. I saw boats from nearly every agency in the tri-state area’s harbor assisting with rescue efforts. The cooperation across state lines and agencies was impressive and showed the strength of the regional response.

I was part of the United States Park Police during the September 11 response, and we played a vital role in marine operations. Some Park Police boats provided security for Ellis Island and Liberty Island. The Park Police worked closely with other agencies to support rescue, security, and recovery missions in the harbor and around Manhattan.

Captain Lauro was one of the people giving us directions during the boat operations on September 11. We received orders from our dispatcher and marine units, including Captain Lauro, who helped coordinate missions like transporting Secret Service agents and moving supplies. His leadership was important for managing the complex tasks on the water that day.

On September 11, we transported agents from the Secret Service who were stationed at Chelsea Piers. We moved them by boat to Ellis Island as part of the security and support operations. The Secret Service was actively involved in protecting key sites and personnel during the crisis.

Chelsea Piers was one of the locations where Secret Service agents were stationed on September 11. We picked up these agents by boat and transported them to Ellis Island to support security efforts. Chelsea Piers was part of the broader network of sites involved in the emergency response around Manhattan’s waterfront.

On either September 14th or 15th, while assisting with operations at South Cove in Manhattan, we suddenly heard a building collapse nearby. Thousands of rescue workers were digging at Ground Zero and ran toward the water to escape. It was a tense moment, and we quickly helped U.S. Marshals evacuate by boat from that area.

Ground Zero was the site of the World Trade Center collapse, and I was fortunate to be one of the officers sent there to assist with the recovery effort. It was a humbling experience to see the destruction firsthand and to work alongside countless volunteers and rescue workers. We filled buckets with debris and helped as much as we could, knowing the cleanup would take years.

During operations near South Cove, Manhattan, we saw U.S. Marshals running toward the water to escape a building collapse. We helped by motioning them onto our boat, rescuing eight marshals and one contractor just before the collapse. One marshal even fell into the water but was quickly pulled aboard. We then took them to Ellis Island to recover and get fed.

Sergeant Gullino was on the dock at South Cove during the building collapse scare and held a line to help the U.S. Marshals and contractor board our boat safely. His quick action was vital in preventing chaos and ensuring everyone got on the boat without incident. It was a tense moment, but his leadership helped us evacuate the group successfully.

Liberty Island was one of the locations where Park Police boats, including ours, provided security after the September 11 attacks. Along with Ellis Island, it was a key site needing protection due to its symbolic importance and proximity to Manhattan. We helped maintain security and support operations around the island during those critical days.
